title: The good, the bad, and the ugly of the Monroe Doctrine
gist: Ledford traces the Monroe Doctrine from its 1823 enunciation through FDR's Good Neighbour Policy to Cold War interventions, arguing it has defined US statecraft as 'good, bad, and ugly neighbour' and that its hemispheric logic still shapes US strategy today.

insights.claim: The Monroe Doctrine, delivered 2 December 1823, enshrined anti-colonialism, non-interventionism and the non-transfer of colonial possessions, becoming the central tenet of US statecraft.
insights.so_what: Foundational lens for understanding two centuries of US hemispheric policy.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford

insights.claim: For decades the US lacked the military capability to enforce the doctrine; British naval power actually upheld it until the 1890s, as Bismarck scornfully noted.
insights.so_what: A supposedly American doctrine rested on British sea power - a gap between rhetoric and capability.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford

insights.claim: FDR's Good Neighbour Policy renounced the right of unilateral intervention (formalised at the 1933 Pan-American Conference and the 1936 Buenos Aires protocol), turning the doctrine into a collaborative framework that enabled rapid wartime hemispheric mobilisation.
insights.so_what: Shows how abandoning coercion built the trust that delivered hard-power wartime cooperation.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford

insights.claim: From Taft's administration to the Good Neighbour era the US sent armed forces into Latin America and the Caribbean nearly 20 times, exemplifying the 'bad neighbour' phase under Taft and Wilson.
insights.so_what: Quantifies the interventionist record that shapes regional distrust of Washington.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford

insights.claim: The Department of War's current 'Greater North America' concept, spanning Greenland to the Andes, descends directly from FDR's hemisphere-wide defensive perimeter.
insights.so_what: Living strategic geography linking 1940s policy to present US posture, including Greenland interest.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford

themes.theme: Cold War excesses and tragic statecraft

insights.claim: Cold War anti-communism led the US to support right-wing dictatorships, arm guerrillas and precipitate coups (Guatemala, Chile, the Dominican Republic, Grenada), the doctrine's 'ugliest' phase.
insights.so_what: The interventionist legacy that still colours US-Latin American relations.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford

insights.claim: Ledford frames statecraft as inherently tragic: forced to choose between ideals and security, policymakers will elect security, often betraying both and producing unintended consequences.
insights.so_what: A realist heuristic for anticipating US behavior in the hemisphere.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford

insights.claim: During the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, Kennedy and his advisers debated whether to invoke the Monroe Doctrine explicitly against the Soviet Union as a catalyst for action.
insights.so_what: Shows the doctrine's pull even at the peak of nuclear brinkmanship.
insights.speaker: Joseph Ledford
